comments.title: Karmic culmination?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I noticed the tern karmik culmination in last week's Magic Monday. Despite having read quite a few Druid texts and some of Dion Fortune's works I had never heard about this before. Could you explain it briefly or if it is too complex for that, recommend a text?
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content: When somebody takes up serious spiritual practice, it fairly often happens that any issues they've been trying to avoid in their life come boiling up in a big way, so that they have to deal with those issues. That's karmic culmination. It's not inevitable, and how difficult it is depends on how much stuff you haven't dealt with yet -- some people have a hard time, some people much less so.

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content:
Different poster.

I found I had something different but maybe in the same vein? I left a Buddhist religious order that I would classify as almost cult like (very controlling of their members to the point that in the home country of the temple in Asia they arrange marriages to lock in families for generations) who extorted their members to never use magic and over the subsequent 5 years things steadily got difficult for me (multiple miscarriages, multiple deaths in the family over a 9 month period - one of which was a punch to the gut, my partner unable to get a job, a very leaky flat, it went on and on). Previously I had been a weirdly lucky person, so this did blindside me. I started my Druid practice and praying to Brighid. Then, 6 months later I had an amazing 6 week period:I was pregnant (I could tell it would keep), I had a new flat, I had a better role at work away from a boss I didn't like and my partner had a job.

It felt like I had needed all the karmic culmination to right myself again. What would the occult take be on that experience?

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content: Some corrupt spiritual organizations curse people who quit. That's very likely what happened to you. Once you came under the protection of another tradition and a different deity, the curse no longer had any power over you.

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content: Nah, there's a difference between a curse and karmic blowback.

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content:
Hm... from what you just wrote it seems to me that one way into serious spiritual practice could be to jump in head first no matter what may show up but that a more controlled (but maybe much slower?) process might be possible too? So... is there a way to get at least a rough estimate of what you would be heading into? And is it possible (and sensible?) to carefully dismantle that heap of karma? How would you do that? That considerations might be especially necessary if you live in a family or in another community where you are heavily involved!?

Thank you,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Karmic culmination?
replies.content:
If you're not doing systematic spiritual practice, you don't have the skills necessary to sense what you've got waiting for you, and if you do, you've already dealt with it. ;-)

If you know a good diviner or clairvoyant, they might be able to give you some sense of what kind of karmic culmination you'll get. In most cases it's pretty minor, and when it's not, you really, truly do need to get something cleared away.

replies.title: (no subject)

1) Good question. I haven't studied ingress charts for the southern hemisphere so I don't happen to know.

2) It's not a matter of "how much," it's a matter of what specifically the retrograde condition communicates. A lot of people these days are frankly superstitious about retrogrades; they don't have to be difficult -- I do my best editing and revising during Mercury retrogrades, for example. A planet that is retrograde at the moment of an ingress represents a force that is backing up, returning or reorienting or regrouping; you figure out what it represents by seeing what houses it rules.

replies.title: Re: Major change
replies.content: I take my ingress charts one at a time; I haven't yet cast a chart for the Libra 2019 ingress, much less anything further out. At least in theory, it should be possible to run a series of ingress charts and find the next one where planets predict sudden change, but it would be a lot of work and I have other things to do; if it interests you, you might consider picking up a good book or two on mundane astrology and giving it a shot yourself.

replies.title: Re: Major change
replies.content:
In theory, this should be possible. You could cast ingresses for a bunch of years (with the partial exception of the Moon, the planets don't actually change position that much over the 1-3 days it takes for the entire world to pass into a new cardinal sign ingress, so you don't have to cast that many charts for each ingress) and see if any ingresses stand out - this is hard to describe, it's something which I do at a level that isn't conscious, but you're basically looking at the overall shape of the chart and how heavily the planets are aspecting each other. (Past examples include 1994 Aries and 2001 Cancer*.) There's also charts which might not be obviously big events in and of themselves but punch above their weight because of the houses they fall in - 1929 Aries is an example, where ruling Saturn in the second, Uranus in the fifth, and fallen Mars in the eighth do unfortunate things to the economy - which you can do by running national ingresses ahead of time. There's also transits to foundation charts, but I'm not good at transits yet.

I will concur with the previous poster that 2020 Aries looks very much like a major event chart and add the even more ominous 2020 Cancer chart, and by "major event" I mean I'd consider both the worst economic crisis in at least a hundred years** and China starting WW3 next year to be live possibilities. (Which isn't to say that those are probable events by any means, but if something like either of those events was going to happen 2020 Aries and Cancer are the kind of chart I would expect for them.) US 2021 Aries may be an example of the second kind of noteworthy chart; it's not obviously a big deal on its own, but it has Mars conjunct Neptune in the fourth and at least half of the chart is aspecting Sibly points.

* - Side note: I only ran the 2001 Cancer chart fairly recently, and would consider it a pretty decent point of evidence that you have to run all four ingress charts every year and overlap them and the difference is how long they're good for. 2001 Cancer is pretty clearly the 9/11 chart, but 2001 Aries had fixed sign Taurus rising for the US!
** - Yes, the Great Depression was less than a hundred years ago. Why do you ask?)

I have a question: Am I right to assume that, when Venus and Saturn are in opposition in a natal horoscope, it means limits and restrictions to the love life of the native? Or are the other influences or aspects in that chart overshadowing the Saturn-Venus aspect alone?

Curiously, I intended to ask about a comparison of karmic culmination and the unloading of all karma in a single lifetime, but another commenter asked first about karmic culmination.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on the chart. How strong are Venus and Saturn? What houses are they in, and what houses do they rule? What planets rule the fifth house of romance and the seventh house of marriage, how strong are they, where are they located, and do they form aspects with Saturn and/or Venus, or with the planets that rule the houses where Saturn and Venus are located? If you have a strong Venus in a good aspect to the ruler of the seventh, and Saturn is weak and isolated, it could mean that your love life will interfere with the limits and restrictions you put on yourself!

Natal charts are whole systems. You can't just pull one pair of planets out of context and say "this means that."

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Sure. Get your own natal chart and go through every planetary position, every house placement, and every aspect one at a time, thinking about what they mean and how they relate to what you know of your personality. Then ask friends, family members, etc. for their permission to study their charts, and do the same thing. Close study of charts is the only way to really learn astrology. Fortunately, you've got your chart, so you have someplace to begin...

replies.content:
3) Making your own is not cheap or convenient, but it can be done. There are many historical recipes in old formularies found on Google Books, and this is a pretty typical example.

8 parts bergamot essential oil (get 'FCF-free' aka 'bergapten-free' which has had rash-causing toxins removed)
5 parts lavender essential oil
3 parts geranium essential oil
3 parts lemon essential oil
1 part clove essential oil
EITHER:
a) 300 parts 190-proof Everclear or 200-proof perfumer's denatured alcohol + 100 parts distilled water added after filtering (see below)
OR
b) 400 parts Bacardi 151 or 151-proof Everclear, no water

If you're pinching pennies, replace the lemon portion with more bergamot and the geranium portion with more lavender.

Be sure you're getting real essential oils, not fragrance or 'scented' oils. The real ones will be thin-textured like alcohol, smell strong and a bit harsh when neat, and each type in the same product line will be priced differently. 'Fragrance' oils are generally thicker-textured, smell more 'perfumey' right in the bottle, and are more likely to all be priced the same. If it doesn't say 'essential oil' on the bottle it probably isn't.

You can get Everclear from liquor stores or duty-free shops in some parts of America, or perfumer's alcohol by ordering online (Google it). It's expensive but if you can't get something of at least ~75% alcohol this recipe is not for you. Vodka won't dissolve the oils properly and isopropyl will smell bad. Neither oil nor glycerine will work for this recipe. Definitely don't use plain water - even if you shake it up thoroughly before use, you'll just get splattered globs of neat essential oil and that can burn skin or etch plastics/varnishes. (This is also why you don't dribble essential oils into bathwater - it just floats to the surface and then your rash has an interesting marbled pattern ...)

Mix thoroughly, let settle a week or so and then filter it through a coffee filter. That step is important because whatever didn't dissolve into the alcohol will be filtered out. Then add the water if you're using the higher-proof alcohol in option A. It may smell a bit rough at first but should smooth out over time.

comments.title: Buddhism as a spiritual path
comments.content:
John,
As I understand it, Buddhism in general deemphasizes magic, spirits, planes of existence, etc. It does not deny their existence but says they are not important for its goals of spiritual growth and ending suffering, and that over-concern with them is counterproductive. Buddhism says the 'siddhis' (miraculous powers) come naturally with advancement on the path but are not something to be actively sought. Many American Buddhists go a step further, interpreting Buddhism as a materialist practice and that spirits, etc. not only do not matter, they do not even exist.

Also the practice of Buddhist meditation, at least in America, is usually mindfulness, aimed at quieting the mind to free it from attachments and suffering, rather than gaining insights like discursive meditation. Sometimes it is focused on compassion, rather than mindfulness.

John, what do you think of Buddhist practice, as described above, as a spiritual path? Is it complete enough? Is its model of reality too simplified? Does ignoring magic, etc. limit it too much? Does having little or nothing to do with spirits, even in thought, grant protection in any way from spells, malign entities, etc.?

I would appreciate answers to these questions, or if these are not the right questions, please answer those instead.

Thanks,
Thomas

replies.title: Re: Buddhism as a spiritual path
replies.content:
American Buddhism is a very mixed bag. There are good traditional schools that offer something fairly close to a complete system of training and practice, but there's also a lot of Buddhism that's been watered down very badly to make it fit within the narrow limits of modern American materialist rationalism, with fairly bad results. Spirits don't go away just because you pretend that they don't exist, after all, and "mindfulness meditation" stripped of its spiritual context becomes nothing more than a chemical-free sedative.

That is to say, no, I don't think American Buddhism as practiced in its watered-down, de-spiritualized, over-rationalized form is a complete spiritual path; in some cases I'm not sure it's still a spiritual path at all. If Buddhism interests you, I'd encourage you to seek out a teacher who will give you the whole system, not those scraps of it that have been prechewed for the benefit of modern materialist Americans.

replies.title: Re: Buddhism as a spiritual path
replies.content:
Thank you John.

I wonder too about the traditional schools like Zen and Theravada, that they may be water-down or distorted in their own ways. Looking at the wikipedia article on Pre-sectarian Buddhism, it looks complicated.
- Thomas

replies.title: Re: Buddhism as a spiritual path
replies.content:
Hi Thomas,

As a long time practitioner of Buddha Dharma, much of genuine practice is not far removed from what JMG calls magic. A simple repetition of a refuge prayer is acting in ways that are not publicly discussed, but the context is so far removed (and so highly specialised) the similarity is not often seen.

There are schools, particularly esoteric Mahayana and Vajrayana, that deliberately use many methods that JMG would probably recognise. It's just that it tends to be (self) secret, as in one literally cannot comprehend the methods unless it is the right time and right place (as guided by a competent teacher, of which unfortunately there are few). What does become public is so garbled and out of context that for all intents and purposes it may as well be madness.

Now finding a good teacher is very difficult, and sadly there are lot of charlatans that do quite a lot of damage. Of the good teachers, most are almost entirely hidden as the public ones tend to attract a significant number of loony followers.

The only teacher I will publicly recommend is Dzongsar Jamyang Khyentse, and his book 'What Makes You Not a Buddhist' is rather amusing. 'The Guru Drinks Bourbon' goes into quite some detail about ways of (indirectly) finding a genuine teacher. Finally, if you like video his youtube channel is Siddhartha's Intent. Be careful watching videos of him by others, as they are often edited to change meaning or remove context for drama.

(As an aside, one of the most fascinating things I find watching his videos is how different people will take drastically (and often diametrically opposed) views of his words. Quite literally people will only hear what they _want_ to hear, and refuse to accept the challenge of understanding and acting on what is actually being said. For those who listen carefully, he DOES NOT pull any punches).

Kind regards,

Daniel.

replies.title: disease egregores
replies.content:
Gods, orishas and saints who are strongly associated with a particular serious disease go a long way back. These beings are prayed to, propitiated or given offerings for protection or cure, not usually to lay a curse. But as a rule any entity that can protect or defend you from a condition also has the power to give you the condition.

Sometimes these beings (St. Lazarus, for example) are depicted as suffering from the disease or ailment or as survivors of it; iconographically, that is the same as saying that they are the representation, force or personality of the disease.

IIRC, there is an Orisha of smallpox. I noticed that it wasn't long after smallpox was eradicated that AIDS became a worldwide plague. It did cross my mind, and I don't know how to express this with the proper respect, that this Orisha to whom many people had formerly made offerings to was rather suddenly out of a job.

comments.title: Geomantic Magic Squares
comments.content:
Question; Am I on the right track with my explorations of geomantic magic squares, and how would I tell? :-)

First, there was some confusion last week about the binary numbers that appear to be associated with the geomantic figures. I updated my blog entry with a diagram of a geomantic diamond magic square that has the geomantic figure in each box vertically, and the binary number for it horizontally. For example, Albus (0010 in binary, or '4') would look about like this, within its diamond:
___o_o___
00_o_o_10
____o____
___o_o___

Second, when you put a 4x4 magic square on its point, so that it is diamond-shaped, you quickly see that the four geomantic figures that are symmetrical from top to bottom belong in the vertical row of diamonds in the middle, These are Via/1111/15, Carcer/1001/9, Conjunctio/0110/6, and Populus/0000/0. If these numbers, 15,9,6,0 appear in that vertical column, in any order, then the magic square in which they appear has geomantic and binary symmetry on every horizontal row in the diamond configuration.

There are 24 possible orderings of these 4 numbers, but if you eliminate the ones that are simply reversed, that takes the number down to twelve.
I am not certain if every one of these 12 sequences appears in at least one magic square--still looking into that-- but I did find that eight of the twelve do appear.

Third--What can you do with these squares?
Last week, I did a geomantic reading on the question, 'Should I study geomantic magic squares?' The 4 mothers were Via, Puer, Cauda draconis, and Caput draconis. If I interpreted the rest of the shield and house charts on that, it looks like there is some benefit.

I have drawn three geomantic diamond magic squares so far. When I put a marker on the locations of the 4 Mothers on each of their locations in the squares, three of them formed a triangle around Carcer, Fortuna Major, Fortuna Minor, and Conjunctio. Puer appears below the triangle.
What I get from that is;
Carcer - It's going to take a long time to work this out.
Conjunctio - Confirms the meeting/joining of magic squares and geomantics.
Fortuna major, Fortuna minor - different benefits to be gained.
Puer - Because excluded, cautions against rash interpretation and early action.

There are probably something like 32 unique geomantic diamonds with complete symmetry. I plan to generate diagrams of all of them and look at the placement of Mothers on them to see if there are any identifiable patterns.

Does that sound like a correct approach? Does anyone have insights on a possible different approach?

For the compulsive, you can see a diagram of one geomantic diamond magic square in my updated blog posting, about half way down, here:

http://jackmanassas.blogspot.com/2019/03/geomantic-magic-squares.html

I would have posted the graphic here, but could not figure out how to link it to my comment. :-\

replies.title: Re: Geomantic Magic Squares
replies.content:
This strikes me as a very good way to work on this, and yes, I think it's well worth pursuing. If you can end up with a unique 4x4 square assigned to each of the 16 geomantic figures, that becomes a matrix of forces that can be used to invoke the energies of each figure in ritual magic. You can use the square itself in a talisman, or you can use it as a matrix from which to extract sigils -- any good book on ceremonial magic will tell you how to do that.

So keep going and see where it leads you!

comments.title: Removing negative beliefs
comments.content:
JMG,
Do you have any magical or psychospiritual advice for removing deep-seated negative beliefs about one’s self, such as “I am an idiot”, “nobody could possibly love me”, etc.?

replies.title: Re: Removing negative beliefs
replies.content:
Yes. The first thing to do is to start learning to catch yourself when you're engaging in that kind of negative self-talk. When you find yourself thinking that, stop and label it mentally: "Oh, that's just negative self-talk." That's all it is, a habit of thinking, which you can change.

The next thing to do is to replace it with positive affirmations. A lot of people think of those as kind of old-fashioned and dowdy, but they're very useful little tools for changing self-concept. An affirmation should have no negative words in it ("no," "not," that sort of thing) and it should express the sense of self you want to attain. You don't have to believe it; you just have to repeat it. Choose one and start repeating it whenever you have trouble with that sort of negative self-talk.

You might consider getting a little further into the old New Thought techniques if that appeals to you. One option I recommend if that's of interest, not least because it's free of charge, is to explore the Order of Essenes Life Science lessons I've put up online. A fair number of people have found them useful in changing negative attitudes to positive ones.

replies.title: Re: Removing negative beliefs
replies.content:
I am one of those people, now working through the second course and having done the first in a little over six months. I can second the recommendation to check it out, since it's fre, and you can always quit after one lesson it you aren't feeling it. The course has been tremendously beneficial for me.

-Kyle

Good day,
Thank you for your helpful insight and advice regarding my sensitive 2 year old. She (and I) are doing well. She is learning how to deal with emotions, whether hers or otherwise. I've long assumed that it has to do with the movement of the branches or some creature moving in them, because she seems to be watching the branches. Even when she's not uneasy, she tends to observe trees quite intently.
Recently she and I had 2 conversations that make me question my assumption.

She points to the building again. "That. It doesnt make any sense."

"Yeah. The building doesnt make any sense."
I'm completely bemused, but go with it. "Okay. So what does make sense?"
She immediately answered "Trees."
"The trees...make sense?"
"Yeah, the trees make sense."
She has, since this conversation, twice more told me that trees make sense.

The second conversation was her cheerily telling me that "These trees are okay."
I repeated that back as a question, and she elaborated "Yeah. These trees are okay. They're not looking at me."

So trees make sense. And they're "okay" when they're not looking at her. And sometimes she doesnt want to approach or walk past them, but the majority of the time she's fine with them.

You've at times brought up communicating with trees. She doesnt talk to them, but maybe she's listening to them. Is that something you'd expect from a toddler? If so, any conjectures as to what makes her so uneasy at times?

I dont suppose the answer will make much practical difference- she seems to be working this out on her own, and is already far less uneasy than she used to be. I don't see this as a problem at all, S.T.

Killing sentient beings, or causing them lasting pain, seems (to me at least) not to be a bug in the "machinery of life," but one of its most basic features. If it were a bug, we could work to eliminate it. As a feature, we cannot eliminate it without wrecking the "machinery." Alas!

I don't know why such a feature should be there in the "machinery of life"; I don't suppose anyone knows for sure, or can know. As Haldane, the once famous biologist, put it, "The universe is not only queerer* that we think, it is queerer than we can think.

[*For Haldane, I should note, the first and primary meaning of "queer" was "strange."]

My own rationalization for this feature is that only one school can teach us the most basic lessons of how to be alive, and that is the School of Hard Knocks. These knocks are mostly administered not by an impersonal universe, but by other creatures. And some of those knocks are very hard indeed, or even lethal.

When you are faced with doing something you find utterly reprehensible, but not doing it would be just as reprehensible, you plumb new depths of humility and learn to think less of oneself and one's deeds, whether good or evil.

This is why Justice must always be tempered with Mercy, and Mercy with Justice.

comments.title: Reiwa
comments.content: Do you think Japanese era names have an egregor?

replies.title: Re: Reiwa
replies.content:
Very much so, and I'm very concerned about this one. The second character in Reiwa can mean "Japan" as well as "harmony," and rei means "order" and "command" as well as "auspicious"; "Japan following orders" wouldn't be a wholly inaccurate translation. The character for wa was also the second character in Showa, the name of the era during which Japan made its last plunge into militarism and overseas empire -- Showa is usually interpreted these days as "enlightened harmony" but it also meant "radiant Japan," cue reference to the Rising Sun flag.

In addition, for the first time in Japan's history, the era name didn't come from the Chinese classics, but from the Manyoshu, the classic anthology of Japanese poetry. Given the elaborately indirect way that Japanese politics handles important pronouncements, I think we've been given all the warning we're going to get that the Japanese government will be taking a hard right turn in the years ahead. That was probably inevitable -- with the US empire in its twilight years, Japan gets to choose between becoming a major military power again, or submitting to Chinese domination -- but the choice of the era name suggests to me that the Japanese government may take to militarism with an unseemly degree of enthusiasm.

Watch for an imperial visit to the Yasukuni Shrine and/or a Japanese military rapprochement with India, Japan's logical ally. If either of those happens in the next few years, the western Pacific is going to be a very interesting place to be not too far thereafter.

comments.title: The Question of Evil for Crowley Day ;-)
comments.content:
I know this is a big ticket in Christian theology, and so I've probably absorbed thoughts on it filtered down through culture that limit how I let myself think about what Evil is (but without the deeper thoughts of actually studying Christian theology), so I'm curious for other perspectives.

As long as I can remember, I have had this deep fear that I was a Bad Person, and trying to be good was a mask. I was raised atheist, so it's not church indoctrination. I am neuroatypical, so I thought part of it could be that I had noticed different rules for different people as a child in a way that made adults angry, but couldn't get the context of social status and -isms until later (e.g. I'd noticed "bullying is bad" is a rule everyone says, but bullying is accepted or encouraged against some people, and those people tend to have similar characteristics) - I thought the angry adults were right and I had done something wrong to say/notice that. I have (obviously) a high tendency for neuroticism and anxiety that runs in the family, so I had eventually just decided my brain gave faulty data, as a nagging concern with being intrinsically bad is a very common preoccupation for anxious people.

But if reincarnation is correct, is it also likely that some of us anxiety cases are actually accurately remembering we were terrible people in a past life? I was certain that my actions would never make up for the intrinsic wrongness of who I am - which, if it was a karmic debt to experience in this life to pay off, seems like it could be counterproductive (like what we do to felons, marking them for life even after release so they can't escape the cycle of crime).

Is there such a thing as just an evil soul? I don't know which is worse, the idea of an Individuality that lasts for aeons of Personalities, all of them Pol Pot and Mao, or the idea that each Individuality could be like that sometimes. Perhaps I have this preoccupation merely to grapple with a Protestant prissiness about evil!

And if people really all have guardian angels to protect and guide us - if Pol Pot prayed, did they answer to help guide his murderous intentions - a dark angel for a dark soul - or did he ignore his "better angels"?

I suppose it ties into the whole questions of magical ethics they seems to keep coming up - maybe we're supposed to remain doubtful of our own intentions - because so many people seem certain of their own goodness and guidance from above, and create such mayhem. So one can do evil if certain they're good, must one first consider the possibility they're evil to do good?

replies.title: Re: The Question of Evil for Crowley Day ;-)
replies.content:
Google 'scrupulosity' and you'll find that a lot of people, and particularly a lot of neuroatypical people, feel the same way. Much of the literature focuses on how this affects religious people, but secular people are not immune. You might find this short series of blog posts (https://thingofthings.wordpress.com/tag/scrupulosity-sequence/) and this more advice-column style series (https://theunitofcaring.tumblr.com/tagged/scrupulosity-cw) helpful from an atheist perspective.

Here's a sort-of-koan that some people find helpful to contemplate: how probable would you estimate it is that you are unusually saintly, morally above-average for a human? However unlikely that is, by definition it is equally unlikely that you're unusual in the opposite way. Tautologically, you're probably no more evil (or good) than the median person.

Sympathies from someone who's been there. Hang in there.

replies.title: Re: The Question of Evil for Crowley Day ;-)
replies.content: Edit: this is a better link for the first blog: https://thingofthings.wordpress.com//?s=scrupulosity&search=Go

replies.title: Re: The Question of Evil for Crowley Day ;-)
replies.content: Every soul gets to experience evil as well as good. "To enter Gwynfydd," says a triad, "it is necessary to be all things, to know all things, and to suffer all things." By the time you get to the point of being able to reflect on moral issues, rather than simply drifting through lives reactively, you've committed atrocities and suffered them. The trap here is thinking that you're worse than other people -- that's a kind of inverse egotism, not unlike the famous non-ego ego trip of the failed Buddhist, "I'm more egoless than you are." "I'm a worse sinner than you are" is just another way of claiming special status. You're human, which means that you incarnated in this form to have the chance to learn wisdom and compassion by experiencing their opposites, from both sides of the equation.

comments.title: Advice on a Grand Cross
comments.content:
I'm an on-again-off-again regular at Ecosophia, but I'm posting anonymously since this is about some personal natal horoscope details I'd not necessarily like associated with me in public.

My natal chart has a Grand Cross with a Moon-Saturn opposition (https://astromatrix.org/Horoscopes/Planet-Aspects/Moon-Opposition-Saturn) and a Mercury-Jupiter opposition (https://astromatrix.org/Horoscopes/Planet-Aspects/Mercury-Opposition-Jupiter). Just to add some flavor, the Moon is loosely conjunct the North Node, and Jupiter is tightly conjunct the Midheaven (and the same way with Saturn/South Node, Mercury/Nadir).

I've linked two articles from Astromatrix on the oppositions, both of which describe me pretty well. The one on Moon-Saturn is the best on the aspect that I've found so far, and makes some sense of Alan Leo's comment that this is possibly the worst aspect one can have.

My main question is this: how does having both of these oppositions in a Grand Cross affect the interpretation? I know many highly successful people have Grand Crosses, such as Steve Jobs, but I wouldn't consider myself highly successful or even on the track to become highly successful; and frankly I am a bit afraid to be.

The only thing I can think of is that it means the the two oppositions are working against one another ("at cross-purposes") and the interplay perhaps can be one of my strengths if I let it. Where the Moon-Saturn opposition is trying to keep me afraid and repressed, the Mercury-Jupiter opposition is pushing me to want more; and where the Mercury-Jupiter opposition tends me toward laziness and disorganization, the Moon-Saturn opposition counters this with a law-and-order, duty-and-obligation tendency.

Also, should I take a closer look at the crosses made with the Nodes and Midheaven/Nadir? Or do those not count since the Nodes are always opposed (and same for Midheaven/Nadir)?

replies.title: Re: Advice on a Grand Cross
replies.content: You need to sit down with a good astrologer and have this conversation. As I've noted here repeatedly, an astrological chart is a whole system; without knowing how each of the planets are dignified, what houses they rule, what other aspects are present, etc., etc., details pulled out of context are misleading at best.

replies.title: Re: Advice on a Grand Cross
replies.content: Thank you.  I'd been wanting to ask this question for a while, and I always figured something like this would be the answer.  Maybe this is the kick I need to finally find a good astrologer.

replies.title: Re: Hexafoils
replies.content:
There are a fair number of those really old Christian small protective rituals. In the English-speaking world they are often called "loricas," and they tend to be associated with the Irish and other Celts. "St. Patrick's lorica" is probably the best known of them, but there are others. The people who devised the SoP seem to have been aware of the lorica tradition.

There's a good (brief and basic) wikipedia article on loricas under the heading "Lorica (prayer)." It gives links to articles on specific loricas.
